resources.string:
Battery Monitor will be completely closed, and you won't see it in your status bar anymore. Any home screen widgets will be useless.
 
 If you just want to leave the main window and keep the service running, press Cancel and then just press your device's BACK button to leave the main window.
 
 If you want to hide the notification but keep widgets running, press Cancel and then change the settings to hide the notification.

You have turned off all notifications for Battery Monitor. This will prevent the app from showing any notifications: the main notification and any alarms you have set will all be blocked.
 
 Since Battery Monitor allows you to turn the main notification on or off separately, as well as temporarily disable any alarms you've created, it is strongly recommended that you unblock the app notifications. While they are blocked, it will appear that Battery Monitor is broken.
 
 Please press OK to be taken to the system settings where you can unblock notifications for Battery Monitor.

Battery Monitor is restricted from running correctly. This "battery optimization" is an Android feature designed to restrict poorly designed apps to prevent them from using too much battery in the background.
 
 Battery Monitor, however, is a well-designed app that must run unrestricted in the background in order to function properly. It is extremely efficient and will not reduce your battery life.
 
 Please press OK to be taken to the system settings where you can disable battery optimization for Battery Monitor.
File successfully written to selected location
This project is open source. You can access the source code at https://github.com/tswistak/Battery-Monitor.
Battery Monitor does not collect, store, or transmit any personal information.

This software is distributed in the hope that it will be useful, but WITHOUT ANY WARRANTY; without even the implied warranty of MERCHANTABILITY or FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE.\n \n While I do my best to make sure that it works well and does not cause problems, I am not responsible for any damage or inconvenience it may cause under any circumstance.
Battery Monitor is a fork of Battery Indicator Pro / BatteryBot Pro by Darshan Computing, LLC. Thank you to the original authors and contributors for creating and maintaining the upstream project.
If you have a question, a problem, or a comment, please email me.
Battery Monitor\'s settings are organized into separate pages as described below. Each page of settings has its own help page describing the settings available there.
You have disabled all notifications for this app. Please enable them to access these settings.
You have disabled the main notification for this app. Please enable it to access these settings.
You have disabled all notifications for this app. Please enable them to access your alarms.
You have disabled the alarm notification group for this app. Please enable it to access your alarms.
You have disabled notifications for this alarm type. Please enable them to use this type of alarm.
You can configure which content to show in the pull-down notification.
Choose whether the notification icon shows battery percentage or temperature. Also choose, whether the unit symbol is shown in the icon, and whether charging gets a dedicated icon.
On Android 16+ with Live Updates, you can configure what the status bar chip shows and whether to display the charging emoji.
Advanced settings for enabling mA info

Android does not provide a means for apps to access information about the battery current. However, a hack is available that allows many devices to potentially get this information.
 
 This is not guaranteed to work. It may not work at all or it may appear to work but only result in nonsense values.
 
 Settings to turn on the hack, if possible, and control how it is used are available in this section.
You can have Battery Monitor notify you when your battery level reaches a certain condition (e.g., the battery becomes fully charged, the battery level drops below 20%, and many others).
All other settings are on this page, including logging, temperature unit, autostart, and more.

Your device supports showing the battery percentage as a chip in the status bar. This is enabled by default.
 
 If you prefer a traditional notification icon, you can turn off "Live Updates" in Android's system settings for this app.
 
 Note: Granting this permission may occasionally cause the app to close. If it does, please simply reopen it.

Your device supports showing the battery percentage as a chip in the status bar, but it is currently disabled in system settings.
 
 Enabling it is recommended for the best experience. If you prefer a traditional notification icon, you can leave it disabled.
 
 Note: Granting this permission may occasionally cause the app to close. If it does, please simply reopen it.

Battery Monitor needs your permission to show the battery level in the status bar and notify you about alarms.\n \n Note: Granting this permission may occasionally cause the app to close. If it does, please simply reopen it.
Choose when the Live Update chip should be shown in the status bar.
Show the main notification while Live Update is active?
If enabled, the main notification remains visible in addition to the status bar chip.
Choose what value to show in the promoted-notification chip.
Show a lightning emoji when charging or fully charged?
If enabled, the status bar chip will show a lightning emoji next to the chip value while charging or when fully charged.

Configure what goes in the top line of the notification: estimated time remaining (configurable elsewhere), how long since the status has changed (plugged in, unplugged, or fully charged), or the battery vital signs (battery health, temperature, and voltage).
You have the same options for the bottom line as for the top line.

Devices come in all shapes and sizes, so most translations need to use abbreviations to ensure that the time-remaining estimate fits comfortably in the notification area. If it still doesn\'t fit, you can opt for the condensed format. If you have plenty of room, you might prefer the verbose setting.
Include Time Since Status Change in Vital Signs?
If set, the vital signs line will also include the number of hours since the device became unplugged, plugged in, or fully charged.
Choose whether the status bar icon shows battery percentage or battery temperature. Temperature follows your selected unit.
If enabled, the icon shows a percent sign for battery percentage and a degree symbol for battery temperature. Disable it to show only the number.
Sorry, the current (mA) hack is not supported on this device.
Attempt to enable the current (mA) hack?
When this setting is enabled, the other options regarding the hack will be usable.

Starting in Lollipop (Android 5.0), Android now provides a somewhat less hackish way to access current (mA) information, directly from the system BatteryManager. However, it is still not guaranteed to work at all or to work well. Until Android Nougat (Android 7.0), the old (filesystem-based) hack method seems to work better on most devices, so it remains the default on devices running less than Android 7.0.\n \n This setting is just a hint to the hack about which method to prefer; you can try changing it if the hack isn\'t working well on your device.

Some devices report milliamperes rather than microamperes as the API calls for. Same devices swap the sign of the value reported. Some users prefer the opposite sign of correctly reported values. To help accommodate situations like these, you can multiply the result of the API call by the value here.\n \n If your device always displays 0 mA, for example, you could try using a multiplier of 1000. If the API call returns 450 microamperes, that multiplier will get the app to show the value as 450 mA rather than 0 mA. If the API call returns 0 microamperes, of course, the multiplier won\'t change the final display value of 0 mA.
Display current (mA) in main notification vital stats?

When enabled, the hack will be attempted and will display the result in the main notification vital stats line. Note that you must have the main notification configured to display vital stats in order to see this. This value will only refresh when the system sends new battery information.\n \n On most devices, a negative value indicates a net drain from the battery and a positive value indicates that the battery is being charged. Not all devices generate values this way, however.

When enabled, the hack will be attempted and will display the result in the main window. This value will be refreshed whenever the system sends new battery information, but you can refresh the value at any time by simply tapping the displayed value. (Note that some devices rarely or never update the underlying data, so the value may not appear to change on tap.)\n \n On most devices, a negative value indicates a net drain from the battery and a positive value indicates that the battery is being charged. Not all devices generate values this way, however.
Try to use current_avg in main notification vital stats?
Try to use current_avg in main window?

Some systems have a further hack available, giving a recent average of the current rather than an instantaneous reading of it. What this means varies from device to device and is only available on some devices. This may give a more stable value (if all goes well), it may return the same value, or it may return an unusable value (in which case you should turn this option off).

If you have the hack set to display mA in the main window, you can refresh the value by simply tapping it. If you want to see frequent updates without having to tap, you can enable this option to auto-refresh the value every two seconds while the window is visible.\n \n Note that some devices rarely or never update the underlying data, so the value may not appear to change even though the app is refreshing the displayed value to match the data retrieved from the system.
Alter icon to indicate when charging?
Icon can show a lightning bolt below the number during charging. Disable this setting if you would prefer the same icon all the time.
If enabled, Battery Monitor will log battery state changes.
If logging is enabled, then whenever you plug in your device, logs older than this will be deleted. Select "Never delete" to keep logs until you clear them manually.
Android reports the battery temperature in degrees Celsius. If you prefer to see the battery temperature in degrees Fahrenheit, check this option, and the app will convert for you.
Start Battery Monitor when device boots:

Controls whether Battery Monitor should start itself automatically when the device boots up.
 
 The "If Running on Shutdown" option works with regard to whether you've most recently manually started or stopped Battery Monitor. So if you kill Battery Monitor through some other means, like a task killer, then Battery Monitor doesn't know you stopped it, and it will still start at boot up if you have it set to this.
 
 NOTE: Android will always start Battery Monitor on boot if you have an Battery Monitor desktop widgets. In that case this settings is irrelevant.

Here you can select how Battery Monitor will predict your remaining battery life / time until fully charged.
 
 The "Sensitive" algorithm is strictly based on your last five minutes of usage. If your primary concern is "How long will my battery last if I keep doing what I'm doing?" then this is the algorithm for you.
 
 The "Conservative" algorithm is the default. It is also based on recent usage, but it tries not to vary from your long-term average too freely.
 
 Select "Since Status Change" to have the prediction based strictly on your current session.
 
 Finally, you can have the prediction based solely on your long-term average usage.
Adds an Advanced tab with battery diagnostics that require either root access or Shizuku.
Root access or Shizuku is needed to show advanced battery stats.

This page allows you to add new alarms, quickly enable or disable existing alarms, or delete alarms.
 
 To add a new alarm, press "Add alarm" — this will create a new alarm and take you to the alarm settings page, where you can control the settings for the new alarm. See the help on that page for more details.
 
 Below the "Add alarm" button is a list of any alarms you have created.
 
 • To edit an existing alarm, just press it.
 • To delete an alarm, long-press it, then select "Delete Alarm". (You can also delete an alarm from the individual alarm settings page by pressing MENU then "Delete Alarm".)
 • To easily toggle whether an alarm is enabled, press the toggle switch to the side of the alarm text. (You can also enable or disable an alarm from the individual alarm settings page.)
If you want to temporarily disable an alarm without having to recreate it when you want it again, you can turn it off and on either here or on the main alarms page.
This is where you can control what conditions trigger the alarm.

There are currently five types of alarm that you can choose from.
 
 The "Becomes fully charged" alarm will notify you when the status of your battery changes from "Charging" to "Fully Charged". Note that on many devices, this isn't necessarily the same thing as being at 100% charge.
 
 The charge and temperature alarms require you to set a threshold below, after choosing that alarm type.
 
 The "Health Fails" alarm will notify you if your battery health becomes something other than "Unknown" or "Healthy", as reported by Android.
Not used for this alarm type
For charge and temperature alarms, you need to set the temperature or charge level that will trigger the alarm.
No advanced battery stats are available on this device right now.

The Advanced tab shows extra battery diagnostics collected from Android system services and kernel power-supply files. Availability depends on the device, Android build, charger IC, fuel-gauge chip, and what the manufacturer exposes.

Counters contain live or recently sampled values such as charge counter, current, energy counter, and cycle count. Some devices expose only a subset, and units may come directly from the hardware driver.

Capacity shows full-charge capacity, design capacity, and estimated health. Estimated health is calculated as full-charge capacity divided by design capacity, so it is only as accurate as the values reported by the device.

Charging shows values related to the active charging path, such as maximum charging current or voltage, charging policy, charging state, and capacity level. These fields may change between chargers and charging modes.

Android battery service shows raw fields reported by `dumpsys battery`. These are framework-level values that often include charging source, battery status, temperature, voltage, and other service state.

Kernel power-supply files show raw one-line values read from `/sys/class/power_supply`, such as battery, BMS, USB, or wireless charger nodes. This is intentionally low-level and may include driver-specific names that are useful mainly for debugging.

Metadata shows higher-level battery properties exposed through BatteryManager, such as state of health, remaining charge time, manufacturing details, or replacement status. Most of these fields require privileged access and many devices expose only a small subset.

If the tab is empty, first make sure Advanced stats are enabled in Settings. On rooted devices, Battery Monitor tries `su` first. On non-rooted devices Shizuku must be installed, running, and Battery Monitor must be granted permission inside Shizuku. Some devices expose only a few fields, which means the missing data is not available through Android or the kernel on that device.
Save current settings to a file
This file was created by a newer version of the app. Importing it may cause unexpected behaviour. Continue anyway?
